case: If in a patient with chronic heart failure we detect prominent v waves in the jugular venous pulse and in cardiac auscultation a holosystolic murmur is heard in the area of the xifoid appendix that is accentuated with deep inspiration. What is the valvulopathy responsible for this physical examination?:
first_argument: - Correct option 3: Tricuspid insufficiency causes #heart failure#, #prominent v waves in the jugular venous pulse# and a #holosistolic murmur in the area of the xifoid appendix that is accentuated with deep inspiration#. - Wrong option 1: Mitral insufficiency would not cause @ondas v prominent in the jugular venous pulse@ and although it causes a systolic murmur is usually in the axillary region (not @in the area of the xifoid appendage@) and is not associated with an increase in breath during inspiration (@emphasis accentuated with deep inspiration@). - Wrong option 2: Pulmonary insufficiency causes a diastolic murmur (not @holosistolic@) in the upper left parasternal area (not @in the area of the xifoid appendage@). Furthermore, it would not provoke @ondas v prominent in the @ondas v. venous jugular pulse@. - Wrong option 4: aortic insufficiency causes diastolic murmur (not @holosistolic@) in the upper right parasternal area (not @in the area of the xifoid appendage@). Furthermore, it would not provoke @ondas v prominent in the jugular venous pulse or an increase in the breath during inspiration (@emphasis with deep inspiration@) - Wrong option 5: although aortic stenosis causes a systolic murmur this is not usually @holosistolic@ and is usually ausculted in the upper right parasternal area (not @in the area of the xifoid appendage@). Furthermore, it would not provoke @ondas v prominent in the jugular venous pulse or an increase in the breath during inspiration (@emphasis with deep inspiration@)

neutralized_argumentation:
Option 3 is correct. The presence of #heart failure#, #prominent v waves in the jugular venous pulse# and a #holosistolic murmur in the area of the xifoid appendix would indicate tricuspid insufficiency. While options 1, 2 and 5 are incorrect. Mitral, Pulmonary, and Aortic valve insufficiencies do not cause these findings.
The correct diagnosis is tricuspid insufficiency leading to heart failure due to a right-sided heart failure mechanism. The presence of prominent v waves in the jugular venous pulse and holosistolic murmur are specific signs of this diagnosis, given its location in the area of the xifoid appendix which is an anatomical landmark.

case: A 30-year-old male with a family history of a father who died of colon cancer at age 38. A colonoscopy is performed that shows hundreds of adenomas throughout the colon. Which of the following statements is FALSA?:
options: The patient has a familiar adenomatous polyposis.
The most appropriate management is sulindac, annual surveillance colonoscopy and total colectomy at age 40.
If you do not undergo surgical treatment almost certainly, the patient will develop colorectal cancer.
The first-degree family members of the patient should be studied.
The patient's children are at 50% risk for the same disease.
correct_option: El manejo más apropiado es sulindac, colonoscopias de vigilancia anuales y colectomía total a los 40 años.
first_answered_option: El manejo más apropiado es sulindac, colonoscopias de vigilancia anuales y colectomía total a los 40 años.
first_argument: Correct 2: family history of first degree colon cancer at an early age and the presence of hundreds of adenomas in the colon suggest a family adenomatous polyposis #family history of a father who died from colon cancer at 38 years old#(support)# #hundreds of adenomas throughout the colon#(support)#. It is an inherited genetic disease in which there is a very great predisposition to develop colon cancer at a very early age. That is why total colectomy must be performed before 40 years of age #total colectomy at 40 years old#(support)# Incorrect 1: family history of first degree colon cancer at an early age and the presence of hundreds of adenomas in the colon suggest a family adenomatous polyposis #antecent family member of a father who died of colon cancer at age 38# (discarded)# # hundred adenomas throughout the colon# (discarded)# Incorrect 3: family adenomatous polyposis is a genetic hereditary disease in which there is a great willingness to develop colon cancer at a very early age so that a total prophylactic colectomy is usually performed #Not to undergo surgical treatment almost certainly the patient will develop colorectal cancer#(discarded)# Incorrect 4: family history of first degree colon cancer at an early age and the presence of hundreds of adenomas in the colon suggest a family adenomatous polyposis #family history of father who died of colon cancer at age 38#(supported)# #hundreds of adenomas throughout the colon#(discarded)#. It is an autosomal dominant hereditary disease so #First-degree family members of the patient should be studied#(discarded)#Incorrect 5:Family adenomatous polyposis is an autosomal dominant hereditary disease so #The patient's children are at 50% risk of the same disease#(discarded)#

neutralized_argumentation: This is a case of familial adenomatous polyposis, specifically an autosomal dominant inheritance. The presence of hundreds of adenomas throughout the colon suggests this diagnosis. The patient's first-degree family members should be evaluated for this condition. It is important to perform total colectomy before age 40 due to the high risk of colorectal cancer in these patients.
